my_thoughts: 'I like the view'—pure sarcasm. They're done with the behavioral tracking, can't stay passive, so they break into sarcastic resistance. The teacher reads this perfectly: don't engage the sarcasm ('Actually the view is...' would be trap), shift to meta-level. 'How involved are you?' changes game from 'notice your avoidance' to 'evaluate your engagement.' This is brilliant—puts responsibility on THEM to assess. The sarcasm revealed low involvement; now make them SAY it. Hypothesis: they'll claim high involvement (can't admit low), then teacher will use their own standards against them.
accumulated_insight: T4: Student breaks into sarcasm: '[Looks back...slouches] I like the view'—resistant boredom replaces passive withdrawal. The slouch + sarcastic comment ('I like the view') shows the relentless tracking is creating pressure. Teacher shifts strategy: from behavioral tracking to meta-level engagement question ('How involved are you here today?'). META_01: shifts from specific behavior to general level of engagement. This is strategic—student can't defend 'I like the view,' so teacher changes frame to involvement. Predicting no_clear_effect because student will likely claim high involvement (defensive).
visible_response: How involved would you say you are here today?
